title: [BUG]: Application crashes with basename context error on page load/fullscreen

Instead of rendering the requested page, the application enters the global error boundary and becomes unusable until the page is reloaded. ### Steps to Reproduce **Steps to Reproduce** Open the application. Navigate through the application (or enter fullscreen if applicable). Observe the application crashing and displaying the error screen. ### Expected Behavior **Expected Behavior** The application should render normally without throwing runtime exceptions. Components relying on React Router should always have access to the required routing context. **Actual Behavior** The application crashes and displays the error boundary with the following error: TypeError: Cannot destructure property 'basename' of 'useContext(...)' as it is null. ### Environment Information Local Dev Environment (npm start / npm run dev) ### Browser & OS Info chrome and firefox on arch linux ### Screenshots/Logs

title: Security: Missing isAdmin guard on PATCH /api/auth/role allows privilege escalation
body: ## Summary The route PATCH /api/auth/role in ackend/src/routes/auth.routes.js:~66 has only uthMiddleware -- no isAdmin guard. Any authenticated user can call this endpoint with { userId: "...", role: "admin" } and escalate to admin. ## Details - **File:** ackend/src/routes/auth.routes.js around line 66 - outer.patch("/role", authMiddleware, authController.updateRole); - Missing isAdmin middleware compared to dmin.routes.js which correctly has both guards - uthController.updateRole accepts { userId, role } and updates without admin verification ## Impact Any authenticated user can become an admin with a single API call. Full privilege escalation. ## Suggested Fix Add the isAdmin middleware: outer.patch("/role", authMiddleware, isAdmin, authController.updateRole); "@ Set-Content -Path "C:\Users\SIDDHS~1\AppData\Local\Temp\sp_ui.md" -Value @" ## Summary In rontend/src/pages/EditorPage.jsx:~156, the preview uses generic alt text that never updates when overlays change. ## Details - **File:** rontend/src/pages/EditorPage.jsx around line 156 - lt="Preview" is hardcoded and never updates - Compliance overlays (face-plot, eye-lines, bounding boxes) change the image content - Screen readers get no information about the image state ## Impact Violates WCAG 1.1.1 (Non-text Content). Screen readers cannot convey what the image contains or what processing state it's in. ## Suggested Fix Use dynamic alt text like ` alt={Passport photo: } ` that reflects the current image state.

title: [SECURITY] Path traversal and missing security validation in upload routes
body:
﻿## Description
The upload route in  ackend/src/routes/upload.routes.js creates its own multer instance with a path traversal vulnerability. The filename is constructed by concatenating Date.now() with the user-supplied  ile.originalname directly, without sanitizing for path traversal sequences like ../.

## Location
- **File**:  ackend/src/routes/upload.routes.js (line 8)
-  ilename: (req, file, cb) => cb(null, \${Date.now()}-\),

## Vulnerable Code
`js
const storage = multer.diskStorage({
  destination: (req, file, cb) => cb(null, 'uploads/'),
  filename: (req, file, cb) => cb(null, ${Date.now()}-),
});
const upload = multer({ storage, limits: { fileSize: 10 * 1024 * 1024 } });
`

### Additional Issue: Security validation chain bypassed
The **comprehensive security validation** from upload.middleware.js (MIME type, extension check, magic bytes, image dimensions, compression ratio) is **NOT applied** to these routes. Only a basic limits: { fileSize: 10MB } is used. Compare with image.controller.js (lines 36-60) which has thorough protections.

## Impact
1. **Path traversal**: A file named ../../malicious.js could write outside the uploads/ directory
2. **Bypassed validation**: No MIME type, magic byte, or dimension checks — attackers can upload arbitrary files
3. **No rate limiting**: These endpoints aren't rate-limited, enabling resource exhaustion

## Expected Fix
Sanitize the filename and apply the existing uploadMiddleware:
`js
const sanitize = (name) => name.replace(/[^a-zA-Z0-9._-]/g, '_');
filename: (req, file, cb) => cb(null, ${Date.now()}-),
`

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Send a POST request to /api/upload/ with a file named ../../evil.exe
2. Observe the file saved with the traversal path intact

title: [ELUSoC_2026] Feature: Automate Photo Studio Passport Photo Workflow
body:
## Description
In a typical photo studio, creating a passport-size photo involves several manual steps: capturing the image, transferring it to a computer, removing the background, cropping/aligning the face to passport standards, and finally printing the result. This manual process takes a considerable amount of time.

This issue aims to automate this entire workflow. By uploading a photo to the Photo Studio workspace, the system will automatically upload the file, send it to the AI background-removal and OpenCV alignment services, apply passport specs (dimensions, centering), and return a print-ready file within seconds.

## Scope & Tasks
- [x] Integrate `usePhotoUpload` hook into `PhotoStudio.jsx` to automatically upload selected files to the server.
- [x] Integrate `useProcessImage` hook to poll `/api/process/job` status asynchronously.
- [x] Connect the "AI Process" button in the toolbar to run the backend processing pipeline with the uploaded filename.
- [x] Dynamically switch the preview area to show the AI processed image once the job finishes.
- [x] Allow users to download the finalized, print-ready image.
- [x] Fix duplicate imports and syntax errors in the frontend (`App.jsx`, `ThemeContext.jsx`, `UploadBox.jsx`) preventing successful production compilation.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Selecting an image automatically triggers background upload to the server.
2. The "AI Process" button triggers the async job using the server-side filename.
3. The preview successfully renders the processed image upon completion.
4. The download button downloads the high-quality processed image instead of the local preview source.
5. The application builds cleanly with `npm run build` without module resolution or syntax errors.

title: [BUG]:I/UX: Refactor Navbar Layout and Update Theme Toggle to Icon Description
body: ### Description of the Bug # UI/UX: Refactor Navbar Layout and Convert Theme Toggle to Icon ## 📝 Description The current mobile navigation bar layout is cluttered toward the center-right, and the theme switcher is currently rendered as a plain text button (`Light`). We need to align the layout to modern web standards by pushing the brand identity to the far left, converting the text button into an interactive icon, and grouping it neatly alongside the hamburger menu on the far right. ### 🔍 Current Behavior - The brand logo and name (`SnapPass AI`) are pushed away from the left edge. - The theme toggle is displayed as static text inside a outlined box (`Light`). - Spacing between the branding, theme toggle, and hamburger menu is uneven. ### Steps to Reproduce ## 🧪 Steps to Reproduce 1. Run the application locally (`npm run dev` or equivalent development command). 2. Open your browser's Developer Tools (`F12` or `Right-Click -> Inspect`). 3. Switch to **Responsive Design Mode** / **Device Toolbar** and select a mobile viewport (e.g., iPhone 12, Pixel 7, or width `< 450px`). 4. Look closely at the global top navigation bar: - Observe that the `SnapPass AI` text and camera logo sit strangely inward from the left boundary. - Observe that the theme toggle appears as a literal text box reading `"Light"`. - Observe that the text box and the hamburger menu button do not sit tightly grouped as standard action buttons on the far right. --- ### Expected Behavior ### 🎯 Expected Behavior 1. **Brand Identity:** The logo (`📷`) and brand name (`SnapPass AI`) must reside on the **leftmost** side of the navbar container. 2. **Theme Switcher:** The text `Light` must be replaced with a dynamic UI icon (e.g., a Sun icon for light mode, Moon icon for dark mode). 3. **Controls Grouping:** The new theme icon must be positioned on the **rightmost** side of the navbar, sitting directly to the **left** of the hamburger menu button. ### Environment Information Local Dev Environment (npm start / npm run dev) ### Browser & OS Info ### Screenshots/Logs _No response_

title: Text color same as background — content invisible on "Passport Requirement Comparator" page
body: ## 🐛 Bug: Text color same as background — content invisible on "Passport Requirement Comparator" page ### 📄 Description On the Passport Requirement Comparator page, most of the text (title, description, checkbox labels, table headers, table rows) is rendered in a color very close to or same as the background color, making the content practically invisible/unreadable. Only the checked checkboxes and search input border are visible. ### 📍 Affected Elements - Page title: `Passport Requirement Comparator` - Subtitle/description text - Checkbox labels (e.g., `Passport Size Photo — India / UK`, `USA Visa`, `Schengen Visa`, etc.) - Comparison table headers (`Country / Standard`, `Dimensions`, `DPI`, `Background`, `Head Size Ratio`, `Eye Position`) - Comparison table row data ### 🔁 Steps to Reproduce 1. Open the Passport Requirement Comparator page 2. Observe the title, checkbox labels, and comparison table 3. Notice the text blends into the background and is unreadable ### ✅ Expected Behavior - All text should have sufficient color contrast against the background (per WCAG guidelines, minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text) - Title, labels, and table content should be clearly readable ### ❌ Actual Behavior - Text color matches/blends with the background color - Page content appears blank/empty except for checkboxes and input border ### 🖼️ Screenshot ### 💡 Suggested Fix - Check CSS text color variables — likely a `color: inherit` or theme variable bug causing text to pick up the background color instead of a proper foreground color - Explicitly set `color` for headings, labels, and table text with proper contrast against the background - Test in both light and dark themes if theming is used ### 🏷️ Labels `bug` `ui` `accessibility` `good first issue` `gssoc26`

title: Email icon and text not properly aligned in footer/contact section
body: ## 🐛 Bug: ### 📄 Description The mail icon and the support email text (`support@snappassai.com`) are not properly aligned/formatted — the icon appears vertically misaligned with the text, and there's inconsistent spacing between the icon and the text. ### 📍 Affected Element - 📧 Email icon + `support@snappassai.com` link (footer/contact section) ### 🔁 Steps to Reproduce 1. Open the website 2. Scroll to the footer/contact section 3. Observe the email icon next to the support email text ### ✅ Expected Behavior - Icon should be vertically centered with the text - Consistent spacing/gap between the icon and text - Icon size should be proportional to the text size ### ❌ Actual Behavior - Icon appears slightly off vertical-center relative to the text - Spacing between icon and text looks inconsistent/cramped ### 🖼️ Screenshot ### 💡 Suggested Fix - Wrap icon and text in a flex container with `align-items: center` - Add a consistent `gap` (e.g., `6px–8px`) between icon and text - Ensure icon `height`/`width` matches text line-height proportionally ### 🏷️ Labels `bug` `ui` `ux` `good first issue` `gssoc26`

title: [ELUSoC_2026] Implement Deep Recursive Request Sanitizer for NoSQL Injection Protection
body:
### Description
Incoming REST request payloads, params, and queries lack comprehensive nested sanitization. Uncleaned fields could potentially allow NoSQL operators or script elements to compromise backend services. We need to implement a deep recursive sanitizer.

### Proposed Solution
1. **Recursive Parser:** Implement utility functions to scrub Mongo operators (`$`, `.`) and script tags from keys and nested values.
2. **App-wide Middleware:** Create a request parser middleware that scrubs `req.body`, `req.query`, and `req.params`.
3. **Mounting:** Apply the sanitizer middleware globally at the main API entrypoint.

### Expected Files Affected
- `backend/src/utils/sanitizerRules.js` (New File)
- `backend/src/middleware/requestSanitizer.middleware.js` (New File)
- `backend/src/routes/index.js` (Mount deep sanitizer in route stack)

title: [ELUSoC_2026] Implement JWT Session Blacklist and Revocation Middleware
body:
### Description
Currently, when a user logs out, their JWT token remains valid until expiration. To secure the system against unauthorized session hijacking, we need to implement a token blacklist that revokes tokens on logout or password reset events.

### Proposed Solution
1. **Blacklist Store:** Design a fast in-memory set that records blacklisted tokens along with their exact expiration times.
2. **Express Middleware:** Build a request guard middleware to inspect authorization headers and reject any blacklisted sessions.
3. **App Integration:** Mount the token blacklist middleware globally for all `/api` endpoints.

### Expected Files Affected
- `backend/src/utils/tokenStore.js` (New File)
- `backend/src/middleware/blacklist.middleware.js` (New File)
- `backend/src/routes/auth.routes.js` (Update auth logout endpoint)
- `backend/src/app.js` (Mount blacklist validation)

### Description
The application currently manages light and dark themes using prop drilling or ad-hoc settings, leading to poor maintainability, lack of seamless updates on routes, and failure to auto-detect system-level dark mode settings. We need a centralized theme provider to manage preferences robustly across components.
### Proposed Solution
1. **Centralized Provider:** Create a dedicated `ThemeContext` providing theme status (`light` / `dark`), dynamic switching functions, and persistence to `localStorage`.
2. **System Preference Auto-Detection:** Implement media queries using `window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')` to listen to OS level preferences.

4. **Context Hook Migration:** Refactor existing views like settings page and admin dashboard to consume this context.

title: perf(redis): add Redis caching middleware with key-based TTL and auto-invalidation
body:
Closes #1166 

**Summary:**
Introduces a full Redis caching layer: client setup in `config/redis.js`, generic get/set/delete helpers, and an Express middleware that transparently caches JSON responses with configurable TTL. Degrades gracefully when Redis is unavailable.

**Changes:**
- `backend/src/config/redis.js` — Redis client creation, `getCache`/`setCache`/`deleteCache` helpers, `isRedisAvailable` checker, connection error handling.
- `backend/src/middleware/cache.middleware.js` — Express middleware that generates cache keys from URL, returns cached responses, and auto-caches on first miss via monkey-patched `res.json`.
- `backend/src/routes/presets.routes.js` — Applied `cacheMiddleware(3600)` to GET `/`.
- `backend/src/routes/uploadHistory.routes.js` — Applied `cacheMiddleware(300)` to GET `/`.

**Performance:**
- First request: normal (DB hit)
- Subsequent requests within TTL: served from Redis (~2-5ms vs ~50-150ms DB)

title: Implement Redis caching middleware for Express API routes
body:
**Description:**

API endpoints currently compute responses on every request, leading to unnecessary latency and database load. We need a reusable Redis caching layer that transparently caches GET responses and invalidates them on mutations.

**Requirements:**
- Create a Redis client wrapper in `backend/src/config/redis.js` with connection lifecycle, error handling, and reconnection logic
- Implement `getCache(key)`, `setCache(key, value, ttl)`, and `deleteCache(key)` helper functions
- Build Express middleware `cacheMiddleware(ttl)` that:
  - Generates a cache key from `req.originalUrl` (optionally with user-specific suffix)
  - Returns cached JSON response on hit
  - Intercepts `res.json` on miss to auto-cache the response
  - Supports a `req.skipCache` flag to bypass
- Integrate middleware on high-traffic GET routes: presets list, history list, testimonials
- Add graceful degradation: if Redis is unavailable, requests pass through normally

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] Redis client initializes without crashing when Redis is not running
- [ ] GET `/api/presets` returns cached data on second request within TTL
- [ ] Cache is invalidated after POST/PUT/DELETE on the same resource
- [ ] `isRedisAvailable()` returns correct readiness state
- [ ] All three cache helpers work with null-safe calls when Redis is down

title: Refactor ThemeContext into a robust provider with persistence and system preference detection
body:
**Description:**

The current theme toggle is scattered across pages with no centralized state management. Theme preference resets on page reload and doesn't respect the user's OS-level dark mode setting.

**Requirements:**
- Create dedicated `ThemeContext` with `ThemeProvider` wrapping the app
- Provider reads initial value from `localStorage('theme')`, falling back to `window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')`, then `'light'`
- Expose `theme` (string: `'light'` | `'dark'`) and `toggleTheme()` via context
- Apply `data-theme` attribute on `<html>` so CSS variables cascade globally
- Listen for `change` event on `matchMedia` to auto-switch when OS preference changes (unless user has explicitly set a preference)
- Persist user's explicit choice to `localStorage` on every toggle

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] `useTheme()` hook returns `{ theme, toggleTheme, isDark }`
- [ ] Theme persists across hard refreshes
- [ ] OS dark mode is detected on first visit
- [ ] Switching OS theme while app is open auto-updates (unless user overrode)
- [ ] All existing pages work with the new context without prop-drilling `darkMode`
- [ ] `data-theme="dark"` is set on `<html>` properly

title: Add client-side image compression utility with configurable quality and dimensions
body:
**Description:**

Users often upload full-resolution images (10+ MB) from their phones, causing slow uploads and wasted bandwidth. We need a client-side compression utility that resizes and compresses images before upload, reducing payload size by 60-80%.

**Requirements:**
- Create `compressImage(file, options)` utility that reads a File via FileReader, draws it onto a `<canvas>`, and exports a compressed Blob/File
- Options: `maxWidth` (default 1920), `maxHeight` (default 1920), `quality` (default 0.85), `resizeScale` (default 1.0)
- Only compress JPEG, PNG, and WebP — return other types as-is
- Add `compressImageWithPreview(file, options)` that returns `{ compressedFile, previewUrl, originalSize, compressedSize }`
- Add `estimateCompressionRatio(file, options)` that returns `{ ratio, savedBytes }` without actually creating a final blob
- All functions return Promises

**Acceptance criteria:**

title: [ELUSoC_2026] Harden Express Error Middleware and Implement Specialized Rate Limits
body:
# Problem
The backend error middleware exposes internal server stack traces in JSON responses across all environments. Additionally, Multer errors (e.g. file size limit exceeded or unexpected fields) are not handled specifically, resulting in raw exceptions falling back to a generic 500 status code with non-actionable error messages. There is also no specialized rate limit protection for heavy upload and image processing operations.
# Current Behavior
- Server leaks stack traces in production JSON response bodies.
- Multer errors return a generic 500 status code.
- General rate limiter is applied identically to lightweight health checks and heavy AI background removal endpoints.
# Why This Improvement Is Needed
Leaking paths and stack traces compromises server security. High-cost CPU/GPU image calculations are vulnerable to denial-of-service attempts without specialized limits.
# Proposed Solution
1. Refactor `error.middleware.js` to catch JWT, validation, SyntaxError, and MulterError codes, mapping them to structured, human-readable JSON payloads. Hides stack traces in production.
2. Update `rateLimit.middleware.js` to export `apiLimiter` and a stricter `uploadLimiter` (max 20 requests per 15 minutes) for upload/process controllers.
# Expected Outcome
- Clearer, safer, and cleaner JSON error messages.
- Reduced server exposure and resource exhaustion mitigation.
# Additional Notes
Limits are disabled during integration tests (`NODE_ENV=test`).

- **Upload Box (`UploadBox.jsx`, `UploadBox.css`)**: Implemented async verification UI. Shows a spinner and disables user inputs during signature/dimension checking. Added accessible `aria-busy` and `.upload-box--validating` styles.

title: [ELUSoC_2026] Enforce Client and Server-Side Verification Pipeline on Image Upload
body:
# Problem
Currently, the client-side uploader only checks basic MIME types and file extensions, bypassing dimension validation. This allows low-resolution images (under 400x400 pixels) to pass through the frontend, resulting in poor-quality, blurry print outcomes when standard A4 passport sheets are scaled at 300 DPI. Additionally, the server-side upload stubs do not validate image buffers properly or return rich image dimensions metadata (width/height) to the client.
# Current Behavior
- Low-resolution images (< 400px) are allowed to proceed to processing without client-side warnings.
- The `UploadBox` component lacks a visual validating state or loader feedback while async signature/dimension verification is running.
- The upload route lacks integrated validation middleware chain constraints.
# Why This Improvement Is Needed
To guarantee professional-grade passport photo sheet printouts at 300 DPI, the pipeline must prevent low-quality, empty, or corrupted files before sending them to the Python AI service.
# Proposed Solution
1. Rewrite `fileValidation.jsx` to enforce a strict resolution gate (minimum 400x400 px, maximum 8000x8000 px) and a 0-byte guard.
2. Refactor `UploadBox.jsx` to execute the full three-stage validation pipeline synchronously/asynchronously, introducing an active `isValidating` UI spinner with `aria-busy` announcement.
3. Update `upload.controller.js` to extract and send back image dimensions (width, height) and correct processing endpoints.
4. Chain `validateImageChain` on the upload route.
# Expected Outcome
- Users get clear, actionable errors when uploading too-small or malformed images.
- Better visual feedback prevents double-submission during validation.
- The backend correctly validates file signatures and returns structured metadata.
# Additional Notes
No breaking changes to the REST API contract.

title: Build useBatchUpload React hook with concurrency control and progress tracking
body:
**Description:**

Uploading multiple photos one-by-one is tedious. We need a reusable React hook that manages a queue of files, uploads them with controlled concurrency, reports per-file progress, and supports abort.

**Requirements:**
- `useBatchUpload(options)` hook returns `{ addFiles, startUpload, abort, reset, results, uploading, progress }`
- Options: `concurrency` (default 3), `compress` (default true), `compressOptions`
- `addFiles(FileList)` enqueues files with unique IDs
- `startUpload(endpoint)` processes the queue with concurrency control via a fixed-size worker pool
- Each file can optionally run through `compressImage` before upload
- `progress` tracks `{ total, completed, failed }`
- `abort()` sets a ref flag that stops the worker pool
- `reset()` clears everything back to initial state
- `results` is an array of `{ id, file, status, error, response }` for each file

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] 10 files uploaded with concurrency 3 show 3 simultaneous requests
- [ ] `abort()` stops in-flight uploads and marks remaining as 'aborted'
- [ ] Progress values are correct after each file completes
- [ ] Compression runs before upload when `compress: true`
- [ ] Hook handles empty file list without error

title: Create ProcessingStatus component with auto-polling for async job progress
body:
**Description:**

Image processing (AI background removal, compliance check) is async and can take 5-30 seconds. Users need a live status indicator that polls the backend and displays the current state: queued, processing, done, or failed.

**Requirements:**
- `ProcessingStatus({ jobId, onStatusChange })` React component
- Displays a colored dot indicator + status label
- Polls `GET /api/process/job/:jobId` every 2 seconds
- Stops polling after 60 attempts (2 minutes) or on terminal status (done/failed)
- `onStatusChange` callback fires with the final status and data
- Four visual states: queued (gray), processing (blue pulse animation), done (green), failed (red)
- Accessible with `role="status"` and `aria-live="polite"`
- Cleanup: clears interval on unmount

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] Component mounts and starts polling immediately
- [ ] Green indicator with "Complete" shown on `status: "done"`
- [ ] Red indicator with error message shown on `status: "failed"`
- [ ] Polling stops after status is terminal
- [ ] Interval is cleared on unmount (no memory leaks)
- [ ] "processing" state has a CSS pulse animation
